I. INTRODUCTION  
Classical static timing analysis, though simple and efﬁcient, has  
several severe drawbacks.  
1) It can be overly pessimistic due to a simpliﬁed model.  
2) It can also be overly optimistic: a chip might fail although static  
timing analysis detects no problem.  
3) It works only for acyclic timing graphs. Only heuristics are  
known for timing graphs with directed cycles.  
4) Arrival times and slacks are not continuous functions of the  
input. Changing an asserted arrival time slightly at some input  
may change a computed arrival time at some output drastically.  
5) Slacks are often too optimistic. A positive slack of x ps does not  
mean (as one would expect) that an additional delay of x ps at  
this place could be tolerated without creating timing violations.  
We argue that the last four mentioned drawbacks will become more  
and more important as extensive minimization of power consumption  
is done because this leads to large parts of the chip with very small  
slack. The ﬁrst point also becomes more important and has to be ad-  
dressed by statistical methods and/or transistor-level timing; however,  
it is not yet clear how to make this effective for large designs.  
In this paper, we will show how to extend the theory of static  
timing analysis using enhanced slew propagation to resolve the other  
four problems. We propose a safe model that is as pessimistic as  
necessary, but not more. It probably works for general timing graphs,  
is continuous, and computes reliable slacks. This is a key basis for  
timing optimization. Moreover, computing the information is almost  
as simple and fast as classical propagation.  
Slack in Static Timing Analysis  
Jens Vygen  
Abstract—The notion of slack is central in static timing analysis and  
very large scale integration (VLSI) design in general. Negative slack means  
that a timing constraint is violated, while a positive slack of x ps is intended  
to mean that an extra delay of x ps (or a smaller delay by x ps in early  
mode) could be tolerated. However, this property does not hold with the  
standard static timing analysis model. The paper deﬁnes slack properly,  
shows how to compute it efﬁciently, and proves that it has the intended  
properties. The proposed idea is based on enhanced slew propagation.  
In the ﬁnal part of the paper, we brieﬂy review slack distribu-  
tion (with many applications in physical design) and show how our  
enhanced timing model extends to optimum slack distributions in a  
natural and well-deﬁned sense studied before.  
Index Terms—Delay budgeting, physical design, slack, slack distribu-  
tion, static timing analysis, VLSI design.  
This is a theoretical paper. The impact in practice will have to be  
determined by future work.  
NOTATION INDEX  
II. STATIC TIMING ANALYSIS

Set of signals at x.  
Signals.  
Arrival time of signal σ ∈ S(x) at x.  
Slew of signal σ ∈ S(x) at x.  
Range of possible slews.  
Timing graph.  
We brieﬂy describe the classical static gate-level timing analysis  
framework essentially introduced by Hitchcock et al. [9], Agrawal [1],  
Jouppi [10], and Ousterhout [15]. We point out several weaknesses and  
generalize the model by enhanced slew propagation in order to remove  
these drawbacks. Based on the generalized model, a new theory of  
static timing analysis is developed.  
Delay function of arc e ∈ E(G).  
Slew function of arc e ∈ E(G).  
A. Classical Propagation  
Manuscript received August 29, 2003; revised March 4, 2004 and June 7,  
2005. This paper was recommended by Associate Editor M. Sarrafzadeh.  
The author is with the Research Institute for Discrete Mathematics,  
University of Bonn, Bonn 53113, Germany.  
In timing analysis, we check that all signals arrive at certain points  
within a prescribed time interval. To do this, we propagate information  
through the netlist.  
Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/TCAD.2005.858348  
0278-0070/$20.00 © 2006 IEEE  
Authorized licensed use limited to: Thapar Institute of Engineering & Technology. Downloaded on March 23,2026 at 17:07:31 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.  
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMPUTER-AIDED DESIGN OF INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS, VOL. 25, NO. 9, SEPTEMBER 2006  
1877  
If all bits at registers and primary inputs remain constant from one  
clock cycle to the next, then the voltage remains constant everywhere  
on the chip. But if at least one bit changes, this information must be  
propagated through the network. The information that we propagate  
is called a signal. A signal consists of: 1) the information whether the  
voltage is rising (the bit changes from 0 to 1) or falling (the bit changes  
from 1 to 0); 2) the time when the voltage change occurs with respect  
to clock cycle 0; 3) a measure of how fast the voltage changes; and  
4) information on the origin of the signal (a primary input or  
register).  
The reason for 1) is the signiﬁcantly different propagation delays.  
This partially makes up for ignoring the logical functions. As both  
rising and falling voltages can occur, we usually have pairs of signals.  
In fact, we normally have four signals at each point as we have  
to consider the earliest and latest possible voltage changes in each  
direction (rising or falling).  
2) and 3) are the standard information in static timing analysis:  
the characteristics of a voltage change over time are encoded by two  
numbers: the arrival time (usually the 50% transition time) and the  
Fig. 1. Arrival times and slews.  
slew (usually the difference of 10% and 90% transition times). In  
the following, we will not restrict ourselves to this speciﬁc deﬁnition  
of arrival time and slew. However, we require that delays depend  
monotonely on slew. This is always the case if the point in time  
considered as arrival time is early enough in the voltage transition.  
All arrival times of signals refer to a speciﬁc clock cycle. Since we  
want to check the timing conditions for the complete chip and for all  
cycles simultaneously, we need information 4) on the origin of the  
signal, in particular its local frequency.  
δ−(v) and δ+(v) denote the sets of arcs entering and leaving vertex  
v, respectively. Let q ∈ V (G) be a vertex with nonzero in-degree  
whose predecessors, if any, have all been processed already [i.e., we  
have deﬁned a set S(p) of signals for each p with (p, q) ∈ δ−(q)].  
Then, we deﬁne a set of signals S(e) for each e = (p, q) ∈ δ−(q)  
as follows. For each signal σ ∈ S(p), we add σꢀ := σe(σ) to S(e)  
and set  
Since the propagation delay of a signal depends on its slew (more  
precisely, on the voltage function over time), we have to propagate  
forward. So for each primary input x and each output pin x of a  
register, we consider a set S(x) := {(x, early, rise), (x, early, fall),  
(x, late, rise), (x, late, fall)} of four possible signals. With each signal  
σ ∈ S(x) in point x, we associate its arrival time at(x, σ) ∈ R and  
some information slew(x, σ) ∈ Λ on the shape of the voltage function.  
We assume that Λ (the slew range) is a ﬁnite closed interval of R.  
Smaller values correspond to “stronger” signals, where the voltage  
goes faster from 0 to 1 (or vice versa).  
at(e, σꢀ) := at(p, σ) + ϑe (slew(p, σ))  
slew(e, σꢀ) := λe (slew(p, σ)) .  
(1)  
In full path propagation, we set S(q) to be the disjoint union  
of all S(e), e ∈ δ−(q), and at(q, σꢀ) := at(e, σꢀ) and slew(q, σꢀ) :=  
slew(e, σꢀ). This recursively deﬁnes multisets of signals, each associ-  
ated with arrival time and slew, at all vertices.  
Propagation is carried out in the so-called timing graph G, a directed  
graph whose vertex set V (G) contains all pins. As usual, the elements  
of the arc set E(G) represent connections within the gates as well as  
wire connections.  
Each arc e = (p, q) ∈ E(G) (also called a propagation segment)  
is associated with two monotonely increasing Lipschitz continuous  
functions ϑe : Λ → R and λe : Λ → Λ. (A function f : Λ → R is  
called Lipschitz continuous if there is an α ≥ 0, the Lipschitz constant,  
such that |f(x) − f(y)| ≤ α|x − y| for all x, y ∈ Λ.) ϑe and λe  
describe the delay and slew at the endpoint of the propagation segment  
as a function of the input slew. In most cases, but not necessarily  
always, the value of ϑe will be positive; it is called the delay of the  
signal along e.  
However, eventually we are interested only in the earliest or latest  
signal from each origin. If a signal is dominated by another one of  
the same type (i.e., earlier arrival time and better slew for late signals,  
or later arrival time and worse slew for early signals), we can safely  
exclude this signal from further propagation. But, if we have two  
signals from the same origin, say on two arcs entering the same vertex,  
and one of the signals has an earlier arrival time but a higher slew  
value than the other, it is not clear which of the two signals will  
arrive earlier at some remote vertex (endpoint); indeed, this can depend  
on the endpoint considered. Fig. 1 shows such an example of two  
rising signals, and their arrival time and slew according to the standard  
interpretations.  
Since full path propagation (even when removing dominated sig-  
nals) can lead to a huge number of signals, a simpler (but less accurate)  
way of propagation is used in practice. For each vertex p, each origin r,  
each η ∈ {early, late}, and each ζ ∈ {rise, fall}, we consider at most  
one signal (r, η, ζ) ∈ S(p). Several signals differing only in arrival  
To allow different delays for different transitions, we use parallel  
arcs and introduce a mapping σe : S → S ∪ {∅} for each arc e, where  
S := Ω × {early, late} × {rise, fall} and Ω is the set of origin labels.  
Usually, σe(r, η, ζ) = ∅ for all r ∈ Ω and all (η, ζ) = (ηꢀ, ζꢀ), for  
some ηꢀ ∈ {early, late}, ζꢀ ∈ {rise, fall} (meaning that the arc is used

only for (ηꢀ, ζꢀ) signals), and σe(r, ηꢀ, ζꢀ) = (r, ηꢀ, ζꢀꢀ) for some ζꢀꢀ

of this net, we then have four parallel arcs (p, q), one for each element

For a start, we omit internal arcs of registers; then, the timing  
graph G is acyclic. So we can propagate in topological order. Let  
at(e, σ) = at(q, σ)}

if σ is a late signal and  
ꢁ
ꢂ
at(q, σ) := min at(e, σ)|e ∈ δ−(q), σ ∈ S(e)  
ꢁ
slew(q, σ) := min slew(e)|e ∈ δ−(q), σ ∈ S(e),  
at(e, σ) = at(q, σ)}  
(3)  
if σ is an early signal.  
However, ignoring all signals except the latest (earliest) one is not  
feasible. To illustrate this, Fig. 2 shows a simple network with three  
primary inputs (black), two primary outputs (grey), two ANDs, and one  
inverter. Fig. 3 shows the corresponding timing graph (we show only  
one out of four parallel arcs).  
Source nodes are represented by black circles, sink nodes by grey  
circles. The mappings ϑe and λe are indicated above and below each  
arc e, respectively, as functions of s ∈ Λ. Moreover, we show arrival  
times and slews at the three primary inputs a, b, c. The required arrival  
time is 70 at both primary outputs l and m. Classical static timing  
analysis would say that this chip works, the arrival time at the critical  
output l is 70, just as required. But the path from c to l takes longer—a  
signal propagated along this path arrives at the output at time 75. Note  
that although delay and slew functions are nonlinear in practice, the  
situation is quite realistic and appears similarly many times on real  
chips whenever two signals arrive almost simultaneously at some point  
and the slightly earlier one has worse slew.  
Classical static timing analysis may thus conclude that timing  
constraints are met although the chip will not work. Moreover, it has  
further disadvantages. It works only in acyclic timing graphs, arrival  
times are not continuous functions of design changes, and slacks are  
often too optimistic. Indeed, the output l in Fig. 2 has slack zero and  
the input c has a positive slack of 4. But if the signal at c arrived 2 units  
later, we would see a negative slack of −6.6 at the output l! This is in  
contrast to the intuitive meaning that the slack is a “safe margin”: an  
additional delay up to this amount should not harm. See also Table I  
Fig. 4. Enhanced slew propagation.

Traditional static timing analysis is equivalent to the case ν = ∞ latches, false paths, and multicycle paths lead to different adjusts. An  
(interpreting ∞ · 0 = 0). However, as shown above, this can be too important implementation detail is that we do not need two different

origin labels for the signals starting in x1 and x2 if adjust(x1, y, η) =  
A safe (but usually much too pessimistic) choice is ν = 0; this is adjust(x2, y, η) for η = early, late and all y that are reachable from  
sometimes called worst slew propagation and is sometimes used in both x1 and x2. This leads to a substantial reduction in memory and  
practice. The following result, which has been obtained independently runtime consumption.  
In a more general timing model, arrival times and required arrival  
times at registers are not ﬁxed in advance. The timing graph is then  
a general directed graph, not necessarily acyclic. We still have arrival  
times and slews for signals S(x) for all vertices x with zero in-degree  
and required arrival times (and slews) at vertices of zero out-degree.  
For other vertices, we may or may not have required arrival times.  
Other timing constraints can be modeled using so-called tests: a test is  
an ordered pair e = ((x, σ), (y, σꢀ)) that represents the constraint that  
at(x, σ) ≤ at(y, σꢀ). For example, each register has at least two tests,  
a setup test and a hold test. After having computed all arrival times,  
the inequalities deﬁned by tests can be checked easily.  
by Lee et al. [13]and Vygen [17,] proposes another safe (and less  
pessimistic) choice.  
Theorem 2.1: Let λ and ϑ be global Lipschitz constants of λe and

ˆ
ϑe over all e ∈ E(G) and over the whole range of Λ, i.e., |λe(s1) −  
ˆ
ˆ
λe(s2)| ≤ λ|s1 − s2| and |ϑe(s1) − ϑe(s2)| ≤ ϑ|s1 − s2| for all  
ˆ
e ∈ E(G) and all s1, s2 ∈ Λ. Suppose that λ < 1.  
ˆ
ˆ
Then, using ν = (1 − λ)/ϑ in (4) and (5) leads to values at(p, σ)  
for each p and each late signal (early signal) σ ∈ S(p) that are at least  
(at most) those of any signal σ propagated to p in full path propagation.  
If G is bipartite, i.e., the source and sink pins alternate on every  
path, we can strengthen the above result (see Theorem 2.3).  
In the following, we ignore adjusts for the sake of a simpler  
notation. Note, however, that they can be included easily without any  
complication.  
Since it is desirable to choose ν as large as possible (while remain-  
ing pessimistic in comparison with full-path propagation), it might be  
better to have an individual number νp for each vertex p; these numbers  
General timing graphs with test arcs allow us to encode that an  
arbitrary signal must arrive before another one. Thus, one can include  
clock signals in timing analysis without having to ﬁx their arrival times  
a priori. Moreover, circuits with complex internal structures, dynamic  
logic, and asynchronous signals can be handled by introducing appro-  
priate tests.  
ˆ
ˆ
should satisfy (1/νp) ≥ ϑe + (λe/νq) for every arc e = (p, q) (see  
Theorem 2.2). For acyclic timing graphs, such values can be computed  
easily by backward propagation. It is even questionable whether the  
maximum has to be taken over the whole range of Λ.  
Note that the above result can also be used to improve full path  
propagation. Given two late signals σ and σꢀ of the same type in  
some vertex q, we can eliminate σ (it is dominated by σꢀ) if at(σ) +  
(1/νq) max{0, slew(σ) − slew(σꢀ)} ≤ at(σꢀ). This may reduce the  
number of signals to be propagated signiﬁcantly. For example, in  
Fig. 4 (for ν = 3), the green signal (number 2) dominates the red one  
(number 1). We shall discuss this again in Section II-D.  
D. General Static Timing Analysis  
The above considerations lead to the following formulation, which  
is general enough to capture all situations arising in common industrial  
design practice.  
Besides the fact that classical static timing analysis (ν = ∞) is too  
optimistic, our model has more advantages. We can handle nonacyclic General Static Timing Analysis Problem  
graphs in a satisfactory way (cf. Theorem 2.5), and we can determine  
the critical parts of a chip more accurately (cf. Theorems 3.1 and  
3.2). Indeed, the more paths are critical (e.g., after minimizing power  
consumption), the more mistaken is the traditional model. Moreover,  
we have a continuous behavior (small changes in the input lead to  
small changes of arrival times), a fact that reﬂects real behavior and  
will prove useful for optimization.  
Note that for ramp functions, ν = 2 in (4) and (5) corresponds  
to the latest (respectively, earliest) 90% transition time (for falling  
signals: 10% transition time). This is essentially the pruning scheme of  
Blaauw et al. [4]. They argued that ν can be chosen to be at least  
2 (see also [13]). Of course, Theorem 2.1 (and its strengthening,  
Theorem 2.3) allows an even better choice.  
INSTANCE:  
1) A ﬁnite set Ω (of origin labels). Deﬁne S := Ω × {early, late} ×  
{rise, fall}.  
2) A directed graph G, whose arcs are called propagation segments.  
3) A binary relation T ⊆ (V (G) × S)2, whose elements are called  
tests.  
4) A compact interval Λ ⊆ R. For each e ∈ E(G), two Lipschitz  
continuous and monotonely increasing functions ϑe : Λ → R  
and λe : Λ → Λ.  
5) For each e ∈ E(G), a mapping σe : S → S ∪ {∅}, such that  
σe(r, η, ζ) ∈(Ω ×{η} ×{rise, fall}) ∪ {∅} for all (r, η, ζ) ∈ S.  
6) For each vertex x ∈ V (G) with zero in-degree: a set S(x) of  
elements of S and numbers at(x, σ) ∈ R, slew(x, σ) ∈ Λ for  
each σ ∈ S(x).  
7) For each vertex x ∈ V (G) with zero out-degree numbers  
rat(x, σ) ∈ R for all σ ∈ S.  
C. Required Arrival Times and Tests  
After having computed arrival times (and slew), we can compare  
them with required arrival times at primary outputs and input pins of  
registers. These are the vertices of the timing graph with zero out-  
degree. So for each pair (x, y) such that y (a primary output or an  
input pin of a register) receives a signal from x (a primary input or an  
output pin of a register), and each σ ∈ S(x), we have to check  
8) A number νx ∈ R+ ∪ {∞} for each x ∈ V (G).  
TASK:  
Find sets S(x) ⊆ S and numbers at(x, σ) ∈ R and slew(x, σ) ∈  
Λ (σ ∈ S(x)) for each vertex x ∈ V (G) with nonzero in-degree, such  
that the following conditions hold, or decide that they do not exist.  
1) For each e = (p, q) ∈ E(G) and σ ∈ S(p) with σꢀ := σe(σ) ∈  
S, we have σꢀ ∈ S(q) and  
at (y, (x, late, ζ)) ≤ rat(y, late, ζ) + adjust(x, y, late)  
at (y, (x, early, ζ)) ≥ rat(y, early, ζ) + adjust(x, y, early). (6)  
1
max {0, λe (slew(p, σ)) − slew(q, σꢀ)}  
νq  
We do not go into detail how the adjusts are computed. In the  
simplest case, when x and y belong to the same clock domain,

In particular, choosing νx = 0 for all x ∈ V (G) is always safe due  
to the monotonicity of the functions ϑe and λe (e ∈ E(G)). As long as  
the numbers νx (x ∈ V (G)) are safe, elimination of dominated signals  
cannot hurt. Of course we want the numbers νx (x ∈ V (G)) to be  
as large as possible as long as they are safe. A possible choice was  
proposed by Theorem 2.1. This result can be generalized as follows.  
Theorem 2.2: Let an instance of the general static timing analysis

If νq = 0 or νq = ∞, (7) and (8)—and similar inequalities in the  
following—are to be interpreted in the sense that they must hold for  
every positive νq or for sufﬁciently large νq, respectively.  
In practice, some delays are often associated with the tests (e.g.,  
setup and hold time). This gives an additional number on one side of  
each inequality (9); this number may depend on the slews of both  
signals involved. However, this situation can easily be transformed  
into the above model by introducing auxiliary propagation segments  
adjacent to each test arc.

We did not include required slews at vertices of zero out-degree in  
our model. These could either be included by strengthening conditions  
(10) and (11), or again, without changing the model, by auxiliary  
propagation segments. Moreover, noninverting loop-back paths and  
common clocktree path pessimism removal can be dealt with within  
the presented model with the usual techniques.

This directly implies safeness.  
ꢀ
This result (in a similar form) can be found in [13]and [17,] and  
implicitly in [4]. We call numbers νx, x ∈ V (G), strongly safe if they  
satisfy (14). Now the following strengthening of Theorem 2.1 follows  
easily.  
Deﬁnition 2.1: Let σ = (r, η, ζ) and σꢀ = (rꢀ, ηꢀ, ζꢀ) be two sig-  
nals at some vertex x. We say that σꢀ dominates σ if r = rꢀ, η = ηꢀ,  
ζ = ζꢀ, and the following holds:

E(G), σ ∈ S(p), and σ = σe(σ) ∈ S(q). The induced subgraph on  
the vertices corresponding to η signals is called the η propagation  
graph (η = early, late).  
It would cause no difﬁculty to introduce two numbers νx for each  
vertex x, one for early signals and one for late signals. However, this  
would not gain much as the optimal Lipschitz constants for late and  
early arcs are similar for practical instances. Therefore, we prefer the  
simpler notation.  
For η ∈ {early, late}, an η propagation sequence is a path in the η  
propagation graph. If the ﬁrst and last vertices coincide, the propaga-  
tion sequence is called cyclic.  
The numbers νx and x ∈ V (G) are safe if for every propagation se-  
We will apply iterative propagation. But let us ﬁrst give a simpler  
problem formulation, without required arrival times and tests. We need  
the following notation.  
quence ((x1, σ1), e1, (x2, σ2), e2, . . . , ek−1, (xk, σk)) (late or early)  
and for any number ai,j, si,j (i = 1, 2, j = 1, . . . , k) with ai,j+1  
=

Deﬁnition 2.3: Let S(x), Sꢀ(x) be sets of elements of S, if σꢀ is a late signal, and  
and let at(x, σ) ∈ R, slew(x, σ) ∈ Λ(σ ∈ S(x)), at(x, σꢀ) ∈ R, and  
at(q, σꢀ) := min {at(e, σ)|(e, σ) ∈ Ξ(q, σꢀ)}  
slew(q, σꢀ) := min {slew(e, σ) + νq (at(e, σ) − at(q, σꢀ)) |  
(e, σ) ∈ Ξ(q, σꢀ)}  
slew(x, σꢀ) ∈ Λ(σꢀ ∈ Sꢀ(x)) be given for each vertex x ∈ V (G).  
We say that S(x), at(x, σ), slew(x, σ) (x ∈ V (G), σ ∈ S(x)) is  
dominated by Sꢀ(x), at(x, σꢀ), slew(x, σꢀ) (x ∈ V (G), σꢀ ∈ Sꢀ(x))  
if for each x ∈ V (G) and each σ ∈ S(x) we have a σꢀ ∈ Sꢀ(x)  
dominating σ.  
(20)  
if σꢀ is an early signal. It is clear that this satisﬁes constraints (17) and  
(18) for arcs entering q.  
Evidently, this deﬁnes a partial order. We look for solutions that are  
minimal with respect to this partial order.  
The claim that none of at(q, σꢀ) and at(q, σꢀ) + (1/νq)slew(q, σꢀ)  
can be chosen smaller (greater) for any late signal (early signal) σꢀ ∈  
S(q) follows easily by induction (again in topological order of the  
Arrival Time Problem  
propagation graph). The running time is easy to check.  
The situation is more difﬁcult for nonacyclic graphs. We proceed in  
the most natural way and iterate the propagation.  
ꢀ
INSTANCE:  
An instance of the general static timing analysis problem, where the  
numbers νx, x ∈ V (G) are strongly safe.  
TASK:  
Find sets S(x) ⊆ S and numbers at(x, σ) ∈ R and slew(x, σ) ∈  
Λ(σ ∈ S(x)) for each vertex x ∈ V (G) with nonzero in-degree such  
that the following conditions hold (or decide that none exists).  
For each e = (p, q) ∈ E(G) and σ ∈ S(p) with σꢀ = σe(σ) ∈ S,  
we have σꢀ ∈ S(q) and  
Iterative Propagation  
INPUT: An instance of the general static timing analysis problem.  
OUTPUT: A feasible solution or the decision that none exists.  
1) Choose a minimal set X ⊆ V (G) such that G − X is acyclic.  
2) Set S(x) := ∅ for x ∈ X.  
3) Propagate in G − δ−(X), i.e., solve the resulting instance of the  
arrival time problem according to Theorem 2.4.  
1
max {0, λe (slew(p, σ)) − slew(q, σꢀ)}  
4) Propagate on δ−(X) and update S(x) and at(x, σ),  
slew(x, σ) (σ ∈ S(x)) for x ∈ X. If at least one of these  
values changes, go to 3).  
νq  
+ at(p, σ) + ϑe (slew(p, σ)) ≤ at(q, σꢀ) (17)  
This gives a sequence of vectors of sets of signals. For general  
values of νx (in particular in the classical case νx = ∞ for all x), this  
sequence has no monotonicity properties, and it is not clear whether it  
converges. However, if the numbers νx, x ∈ V (G), are strongly safe,  
we have some monotonicity, in the sense of Deﬁnition 2.3.  
Theorem 2.5: Let I1 and I2 be two instances of the arrival time  
problem on an acyclic graph, differing only in the information on  
the vertices with zero in-degree. Suppose that I1 dominates I2 with  
respect to the information S(x), at(x, σ), slew(x, σ) (σ ∈ S(x)) for  
vertices x of zero in-degree. Then the solution to I1 dominates the  
solution to I2.  
if σ is a late signal and  
1
min {0, λe (slew(p, σ)) − slew(q, σꢀ)}  
νq  
+ at(p, σ) + ϑe (slew(p, σ)) ≥ at(q, σꢀ) (18)  
if σ is an early signal.  
Moreover, the solution must be dominated by any other such  
solution.  
Proof: Straightforward induction using (15).  
ꢀ
We shall prove that if there is a solution satisfying (17) and (18),  
then there is also one that is dominated by any other such solution.  
Moreover, we shall show how to solve this problem.  
It is clear that solving the arrival time problem is sufﬁcient. Either  
such a solution meets all further constraints (tests and required arrival  
times) speciﬁed in the instance of the general static timing analysis  
problem, or the instance is infeasible.  
Consider the sequence of vectors with entries at(x, σ) indexed  
˜
by the pairs (x, σ) for x ∈ X and σ ∈ S(x). By Theorem 2.5, this  
sequence is monotone. Hence, it is either unbounded (in which case the  
instance of the general static timing analysis problem is infeasible), or  
it converges. (It is a well-known theorem that every bounded monotone  
sequence converges.) If the above sequence converges, the sequence of  
the slews is convergent too. We summarize:  
Theorem 2.6: A feasible instance of the arrival time problem has  
a unique solution. The sequence of signals, arrival times, and slews  
computed by iterative propagation converges to this solution. For an  
infeasible instance of the arrival time problem, it is even impossible to  
satisfy only (17) and (18).  
Either this solution constitutes a feasible solution to the general  
static timing analysis problem, or there is no feasible solution. It is easy  
to construct (artiﬁcial) examples where no feasible solution is found  
after any ﬁnite number of iterations, although one exists. However,  
this is very unlikely in practice. The author’s experience with iterative  
propagation in classical timing analysis on industrial chips suggests  
that solutions of excellent accuracy can be obtained after a very small  
number of iterations.  
E. Solving the Arrival Time Problem  
In this section, we discuss how to solve the arrival time problem.  
Let us start with the acyclicꢀcase. The following notation will prove  
ꢀ
˜
useful. For q ∈ V (G) and σ ∈ S(q), let Ξ(q, σ ) be the set of pairs  
(e, σ) ∈ δG−(q) × S with e = (p, q), σ ∈ S(p), and σe(σ) = σꢀ.  
Theorem 2.4: The arrival time problem can be solved in  
O(|Ω||E(G)|) time if the propagation graph is acyclic.  
Proof: We proceed as indicated in (1), (4), and (5). For each  
pair (e, σ) ∈ Ξ(q, σꢀ), we set at(e, σ) := at(p, σ) + ϑe(slew(p, σ))  
and slew(e, σ) := λe(slew(p, σ)). Then we add only one copy of σꢀ  
to S(q) and set  
at(q, σꢀ) := max {at(e, σ)|(e, σ) ∈ Ξ(q, σꢀ)}  
III. SLACK  
slew(q, σꢀ) := max {slew(e, σ) + νq (at(e, σ) − at(q, σꢀ)) |  
In the design process, it is often useful to know which are the critical  
(19) parts of a chip. In particular, we ask how much additional delay we

(e, σ) ∈ Ξ(q, σꢀ)}

could spend at a certain point without violating any constraint; in other  
Indeed, the above deﬁnitions extend to general timing graphs with-  
words, how much slack a constraint has. We shall now make this notion out any change as long as a feasible solution to the arrival time problem  
precise and prove important properties.  
exists. Required arrival times satisfying (22)–(25) can be computed  
easily by a shortest path computation. We need two prerequisites.  
First, a primary output or a test must be reachable from every vertex.  
This holds unless the vertex is trivially redundant. Second, the sum  
A. New Deﬁnition  
Assume an instance of the general static timing analysis problem. of rat(ei, σi) − rat(vi+1, σi+1), i = 1, . . . , k − 1, as deﬁned by (22),  
For a vertex x of the timing graph, we deﬁne the early slack and late must be nonnegative on every cyclic late propagation sequence  
slack of x by  
((v1, σ1), e1, (v2, σ2), e2, . . . , ek−1, (vk, σk))  
(26)  
slack(x, early) := min {at(x, σ) − rat(x, σ)|σ ∈ S(x) early signal}  
slack(x, late) := min {rat(x, σ) − at(x, σ)|σ ∈ S(x) late signal} .  
(21)  
(and, analogously, nonpositive on every cyclic early propagation  
sequence). However, note that we have for i = 1, . . . , k − 1  
rat(ei, σi) − rat(vi+1, σi+1) ≥ at(vi, σi) − at(vi+1, σi+1  
)
(27)  
[due to (22) and (17)], and the sum of the latter values on a cyclic  
propagation sequence is zero. For early propagation sequences, the  
same argument applies with reversed inequality sign.  
With the Moore–Bellman–Ford shortest path algorithm (see, for  
example, [11]), we get the following.  
Proposition 3.1: Required arrival times, and thus slacks, can be  
computed in O(|V (G)||E(G)| + |T|) time provided that a feasible  
solution to the arrival time problem is given.  
We assume that the arrival times are chosen minimal (maximal) for all  
late (early) signals, as this implies maximal slacks. In other words, we  
assume a solution to the arrival time problem. But deﬁnition, (21) also  
involves the required arrival times; these are computed by a kind of  
backward propagation using the following recursion formulas.  
For a propagation segment e = (p, q), σ ∈ S(p) and σꢀ = σe(σ) ∈  
S(q), we set  
rat(e, σ) := rat(q, σꢀ) − ϑe (slew(p, σ))  
B. Example  
1
−
max {0, λe (slew(p, σ)) − slew(q, σꢀ)} (22)  
Let us consider the example in Fig. 2 again. Table I shows arrival  
times, slews, required arrival times, and late slacks for the 13 pins a–m  
with classical propagation (ν = ∞, columns 2–5) and with enhanced  
slew propagation and the required arrival deﬁnition of Section III-A  
with νx = ν = 3 for all x (columns 6–9).  
All classical slack values are too optimistic in this example. For  
example, increasing the arrival time at input c by 3 units (4 instead  
of 1) and applying classical timing analysis yields the surprising result  
that all slacks, except those at a and g, become suddenly negative!  
Such awkward results cannot occur with the new slacks as proposed  
here, as the model is continuous. Moreover, positive slacks guarantee  
a safe margin within which delay changes cause no harm. This will be  
proved in the next section.  
νq  
if σ is a late signal, and  
rat(e, σ) := rat(q, σꢀ) − ϑe (slew(p, σ))

−
min {0, λe (slew(p, σ)) − slew(q, σꢀ)} (23)  
νq  
if σ is an early signal.  
For a vertex p with nonzero out-degree in G and a late signal σ ∈  
S(p), we set

Note that the notion of critical path does not make sense anymore:  
the subgraph induced by pins with smallest slack value is, in general,  
not a path.

C. Main Properties  
ꢁ
ꢁ
ꢂ
Now we prove the essential properties of the so-deﬁned slacks.  
Lemma 3.1: Let q ∈ V (G) be a vertex with nonzero in-degree and  
σꢀ ∈ S(q). For each (e, σ) ∈ Ξ(q, σꢀ), we have rat(e, σ) − at(p, σ) ≥  
rat(q, σꢀ) − at(q, σꢀ) if e is a late arc, and rat(e, σ) − at(p, σ) ≤  
rat(q, σꢀ) − at(q, σꢀ) if e is an early arc. Moreover, there exists an  
(e, σ) ∈ Ξ(q, σꢀ) with rat(e, σ) − at(p, σ) = rat(q, σꢀ) − at(q, σꢀ).  
Proof: If e is a late arc, we have  
rat(p, σ) := max max rat(e, σ)|e ∈ δ+(p), σe(σ) = ∅  
,
G
max {at(q, σꢀ)| ((q, σꢀ), (p, σ)) ∈ T,  
σꢀ ∈ S(q)}} .  
(25)  
For a vertex p with zero out-degree in G, the numbers rat(p, σ) are  
given as input. If there are tests incident to such a vertex, we take  
the minimum of (24) and the given rat(p, σ), or the maximum of (25)  
and the given rat(p, σ), respectively. If the timing graph is acyclic, this  
recursively deﬁnes required arrival times (and thus slacks) everywhere,  
and they can be computed in linear time.  
Again, for νx = ∞(x ∈ V (G)), this coincides with the classical  
slack computation. But, as noted in Section II-A, these slacks do not  
have the intended property.  
rat(e, σ) − at(p, σ) = rat(q, σꢀ) − at(p, σ) − ϑe (slew(p, σ))

−
max {0, λe (slew(p, σ)) − slew(q, σꢀ)}  
νq  
≥ rat(q, σꢀ) − at(q, σꢀ).  
(28)  
If e is an early arc, we have  
Fortunately, if the numbers νx (x ∈ V (G)) are strongly safe, then  
the slacks as deﬁned above have the intended property. We can add  
as much delay as slack(p, late) in a pin p without making any path  
containing p too slow. Before proving this essential property, we show  
that our concept of slacks also works for nonacyclic timing graphs.  
rat(e, σ) − at(p, σ) = rat(q, σꢀ) − at(p, σ) − ϑe (slew(p, σ))

≤ rat(q, σꢀ) − at(q, σꢀ).

Case 1) q is not reachable from X. Then, (32) holds for each  
predecessor of q, and thus it continues to hold also for q.  
slack(p, late) = rat(p, σ) − at(p, σ)

(1/νq)(slewꢀ(q, σ) − slew(q, σ)), i.e., (33) holds.  
Case 3) q ∈ X, but q is reachable from X. Let (e, σ) ∈ Ξ(q, σꢀ),  
e = (p, q). If p is not reachable from X [and thus satisﬁes

ꢀ
Proposition 3.3: The η slack at a vertex of nonzero in-degree in G  
is at least the minimum η slack of its predecessors (η ∈ {early, late}).  
Proof: Let q be a vertex of nonzero in-degree. Let σꢀ ∈ S(q)  
be a late signal with slack(q, late) = rat(q, σꢀ) − at(q, σꢀ). Let (e =  
(p, q), σ) ∈ Ξ(q, σꢀ) such that rat(e, σ) − at(p, σ) = rat(q, σꢀ) −

Similar for early signals.  
We now prove our main result on slacks.  
ꢀ
Theorem 3.1: Consider a timing graph where all late slacks are  
nonnegative. Let X be a set of vertices such that no propagation  
sequence contains more than one of them (an antichain). Suppose we  
add a delay of d(x) ≤ slack(x, late) at each vertex x ∈ X. Then all  
late slacks with respect to the solution to the modiﬁed instance of the  
arrival time problem remain nonnegative.  
If p be reachable from X [and thus satisﬁes (33)], we have

The above inequalities imply atꢀ(p, σ) ≤ rat(p, σ) everywhere, in

unchanged, Proposition 3.2 then implies the theorem.

We prove (32) and (33) by induction on the steps of iterative  
propagation. We show that the claim holds at any stage. Since the  
arrival times in iterative propagation converge to the unique solution  
of the modiﬁed instance of the arrival time problem by Theorem 2.6,  
this will conclude the proof.

An analogous proof yields the corresponding statement for early  
slacks (we omit the details).

Theorem 3.2: Consider a timing graph where all early slacks are  
nonnegative. Let X be a set of vertices such that no propagation  
sequence contains more than one of them. Suppose we subtract a delay  
of d(x) ≤ slack(x, early) at each vertex x ∈ X. Then all early slacks  
with respect to the solution to the modiﬁed instance of the arrival time  
The motivation is the following easy observation.  
Proposition 3.4: Let (B, c) be the balance graph and π : V (B) →  
R with π(0) = 0. Then, π is a feasible potential of (B, c), i.e., cπ(e):=  
c(e) + π(x) − π(y) ≥ 0 for e = (x, y) ∈ E(B), if and only if after  
replacing arrival times by π((x, σ)), x ∈ V (G), σ ∈ S(x), in the  
solution to the arrival time problem it is (still) a feasible solution to  
problem remain nonnegative.  
ꢀ
Of course, these theorems are not applicable to any vertex that  
lies on a cyclic propagation sequence. The slack of these vertices  
may overestimate the delay that one can safely add at this vertex.  
To correct this, one could replace the late slack by the minimum  
of the above-deﬁned slack and the minimum weight of cyclic late  
propagation sequence containing this vertex, where the weight of an  
arc ((p, σ), (q, σꢀ)) of the late propagation graph is  
the given instance of the general static timing analysis problem.  
ꢀ
We call cπ(e) the independent slack of e = (x, y) ∈ E(B) with  
respect to π. However, several arcs of the balance graph stem from the  
same propagation segment, and there is no point in having different  
slacks on one and the same arc of G. In general, we have a partition  
of the arc set into bunches and ask for a common slack for each  
bunch.  
1
Moreover, we often need independent slacks on a subset of the arcs  
only. Among these, the slacks should be “distributed as equally as  
possible,” possibly proportional to their sensitivity. More precisely,  
we consider the following quite general combinatorial optimization  
problem.  
−ϑe (slew(p, σ)) −  
max {0, λe (slew(p, σ)) − slew(q, σꢀ)}  
νq  
(38)  
where e = (p, q), similar for early slacks.  
D. Slack Distribution  
General Balance Potential Problem  
Often, we need independent slacks on gate arcs only or on net arcs  
only. This means that additional delays up to such late slacks should  
be acceptable simultaneously at all vertices. Of course, these slacks  
should be “distributed as equally as possible,” possibly proportionally  
to their sensitivity. Such a slack distribution has many applications  
in physical design, in particular at later stages (detailed placement,  
routing). See the references at the end of this section.

cπ(e) ≥ 0 for e ∈ E(B) \

where the numbers νx (x ∈ V (G)) are strongly safe, and a solution to  
the corresponding instance of the arrival time problem. We construct  
the following weighted directed graph.

described an O(mn + n2 log n) algorithm, extending the parametric  
Authorized licensed use limited to: Thapar Institute of Engineering & Technology. Downloaded on March 23,2026 at 17:07:31 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.  
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMPUTER-AIDED DESIGN OF INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS, VOL. 25, NO. 9, SEPTEMBER 2006  
1885  
shortest path algorithm of Young et al. [19]. For the generalization to  
arbitrary weights, see [8].  
Other works considered mostly the trivial partition of all singletons.  
If w ≡ 1, this special case reduces to the so-called minimum balance  
problem, which has been considered by Schneider and Schneider [16]  
and Young et al. [19].  
The lexicographic order is the appropriate balance measure, as is  
demonstrated by the following equivalent formulation.  
Theorem 3.3: Let (B, c, F, w) be an instance of the general balance  
potential problem. For π : V (B) → R, let  
IV. CONCLUSION  
Classical static timing analysis is simple and efﬁcient but has several  
major drawbacks: it may conclude that a chip works although it does  
not, results are not continuous functions of the input (in contrast to  
reality), convergence in nonacyclic timing graphs cannot be proved,  
and slacks are often too optimistic. We proposed a modiﬁcation,  
based on enhanced slew propagation, that is just as efﬁcient but has  
none of these drawbacks. In particular, we redeﬁned the notion of  
slack, which is central in very large scale integration (VLSI) design,  
properly. We also addressed how the popular method of distributing  
slack is affected. A thorough experimental study with an industrial  
timing engine and a combination with statistical timing is left for  
future work.

which Eπ is minimal among all optimal solutions if and only if for  
each X ⊆ V (B)

graphs and matrix scaling,” Math. Oper. Res., vol. 16, no. 1, pp. 208–222,

Math., Univ. Bonn, Bonn, Germany, 2001.

cuit with application on FPGA placement,” in Proc. DAC, Anaheim, CA,  
2003, pp. 202–207.

and minimum balance algorithms,” Networks, vol. 21, no. 2, pp. 205–221,

circuits,” IEEE Trans. Circuits Syst. II, Analog Digit. Signal Process.,  
vol. 39, no. 11, pp. 815–824, Nov. 1992.

Note that (42) can be interpreted as a local optimality criterion:  
reducing π on any set X [and thus decreasing cπ(e) for e ∈ δ+(X)  
B
and increasing cπ(e) for e ∈ δ−(X)]makes the situation worse  
B
(cf. Fig. 5).  
Proof: Any optimal solution with minimal Eπ satisﬁes (42);  
otherwise, we could decrease π(x) (x ∈ X) by a sufﬁciently small  
, > 0 and thereby increase the sorted vector (40) lexicographically  
(disproving optimality), or keep this vector identical (maintaining  
optimality) but remove at least one arc from Eπ.  
Let now π, πꢀ : V (B) → R be two vectors with (39) and (42), and

π(q) = πꢀ(p) − πꢀ(q), and choose  
f
such that min{cπ(f),  
ꢀ
cπ (f)}/w(f) is minimum. Without loss of generality, π(p) −  
π(q) < πꢀ(p) − πꢀ(q). Then, f ∈ Eπ: the contrary assumption  
would imply that there exist fꢀ ∈ E(B) and F ∈ F with f,  
fꢀ ∈ F and cπ(fꢀ)/w(fꢀ) < cπ(f)/w(f). Then, cπ (f )/w(f ) =

cπ(fꢀ)/w(fꢀ) < cπ(f)/w(f) < cπ (f)/w(f) and thus f ∈ Eπ  
,

a contradiction.  
Let Q be the set of all vertices reachable from q via arcs e ∈  
ꢀ
E(B) \ F with cπ(e) = 0 or arcs e ∈ Eπ with cπ(e)/w(e) ≤  
cπ(f)/w(f). Then, by (42), we have p ∈ Q. Hence, there is a q–p path  
P that consists of arcs e = (x, y) with c(e) + π(x) − π(y) ≤ c(e) +  
πꢀ(x) − πꢀ(y). Summation yields π(q) − π(p) ≤ πꢀ(q) − πꢀ(p),  
a contradiction.  
ꢀ
Special cases of this theorem have been proved by Schneider and  
Schneider [16]and Albrecht [2].  
Other approaches to slack balancing known from the litera-  
ture are the so-called zero-slack algorithm [14], the extensions by  
Youssef et al. [20], and the algorithm by Chen et al. [7](see also  
[6]) that distributes slacks in order to maximize the sum of all in-  
dependent slacks. They all work for acyclic timing graphs only. See  
also [12]. There are many other papers on slack balancing, or, almost  
equivalently, delay budgeting, with slightly different objectives, new  
applications, and combinations with other design tasks, in particular  
placement or retiming. See [5]and [18]for examples.
